name: RawData+opencog-ml+Looking to contribute
content:
Source: opencog-dev mailing list
Thread: Looking to contribute
Date range: Fri, 13 Nov 2015 04:29:44 -0800 (PST) to Sun, 10 Jan 2016 21:16:52 -0600
Messages: 15
Participants: Arvind C R, Linas Vepstas, Ben Goertzel, Kole, Amen Belayneh
---

From: Arvind C R
Date: Fri, 13 Nov 2015 04:29:44 -0800 (PST)

Hello, 
I am looking to contribute to the opencog. One of the things i find 
interesting is the spatial library. I am currently at ucsb and would prefer 
to work with you from here.

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Sat, 14 Nov 2015 23:11:45 +0200

Hi Arvind,

You can work from anywhere. We don't have any firm p;lans for the future of
the spatial library, mostly because we don't currrently have any users for
it.  The interface to Minecraft does not use it. The robots use ROS tf2
instead. Spatial reasoning is indeed important, but no one is working on
that at the moment.

Hooking up spatial reasoning to language would be very very interesting,
but I don't know who could advise you at  this time.  Maybe I cold, maybe
in a few months, maybe Ben could or maybe Nil....

--linas

---

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Sun, 15 Nov 2015 15:49:11 +0800

Hi Arvind,

Regarding spatial reasoning, I think the best way to start with this would
be to work in a Minecraft context ... a couple GSoC students last summer
finished the "plumbing" to make OpenCog interact with Minecraft agents, but
didn't actually make Minecraft agents that do anything...

A start would be to write some scripts in OpenCog similar to these (which
are for controlling a robot head)

https://github.com/opencog/ros-behavior-scripting

but for controlling a Minecraft agent...

Then, in the context of that agent, one could have the agent do some
spatial and temporal reasoning in Minecraft

Some temporal reasoning rules were coded in the old python version of PLN

https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/opencog/HGCRIHBlnpg

and need to be ported to the new, Unified Rule [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Thu, 7 Jan 2016 23:37:11 -0600

Hi Kole,

TimeNode is very old and not very compatible with the current code base.
There would need to be some work to make it compatible, so that it could be
comparable with a GreaterThan link.

However, I did a quick hack: this pull request:
https://github.com/opencog/atomspace/pull/584
does allow this code to work correctly:

(use-modules (opencog))
(use-modules (opencog exec))
(use-modules (opencog query))
(use-modules (opencog atom-types))

(AtTimeLink
  (TimeNode "8000")
  (EvaluationLink
     (PredicateNode "shower")
  )
)

(define find-shower
   (GetLink
      (VariableList (Variable "$t") (Variable "$X"))
      (And
         (GreaterThan (Time 8300) (Variable "$t"))
         (GreaterThan (Variable "$t") (Time 7700))
         (AtTime (Variable "$t") (Evaluation (Variable "$X")))
  [...]

 (AndLink
  (Precedes a- b-)
  (Precedes b- a+)
  (Precedes a+ b+)
 ))

(define (During a- a+ b- [...]

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Fri, 8 Jan 2016 16:23:21 -0600

Well, realize that GreaterThan is an actual Atom, but Precedes is not.
That said, you can probably use Precedes as if it really were an atom,
although there may be places where they are not quite interchangeable.

This is almost surely use-less in real-life, because equality for floating
point numbers is a very fragile thing.  You can be off by a tiny amount:
10^-15 and it will compare as unequal.  10^-15 seconds is a femto-second. A
CPU running at 3GHz takes a third of a million femtoseconds to clock one
cycle.   So, for floats, you really need a "equal on what time-scale"
predicate.

In particular, in floating point, 1.0 is not equal to 3.0 * (1.0/3.0) due
to rounding errors.

Looks plausible, I guess. Just be aware that when Ben writes Evaluation
 Preceeds $x $y   he [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Sat, 9 Jan 2016 13:34:49 -0600

Perhaps the following solves your problem:

(Implication
   ; Type declarations, to constrain how the rule is applied.
   (VariableList
      (TypedVariable (Variable "$a-") (Type "TimeNode"))
      (TypedVariable (Variable "$a+") (Type "TimeNode"))
      (TypedVariable (Variable "$b-") (Type "TimeNode"))
      (TypedVariable (Variable "$b+") (Type "TimeNode"))
   )
   ; If the numerical relations below hold true...
   (And
      (less (Variable "$a-") (Variable "$a+"))
      (less (Variable "$b-") (Variable "$b+"))
      (less (Variable "$a-") (Variable "$b-"))
      (less (Variable "$b-") (Variable "$a-"))
      (less (Variable "$a+") (Variable "$b+"))
   )
   ; Then create the following predicate...
   (Evaluation
      (PredicateNode "Overlaps")
      (ListLink
         (ListLink [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Sun, 10 Jan 2016 21:16:52 -0600

Hi,

Well, technically, there is a syntax error here, but it seems to be a
common mistake, and pull request #590  works around it.

The problem is this: you did not tell it what to search for!  Thus you
should have said:

(BindLink ... etc....
   (AndLink
       (PresentLink (Variable "$x"))
       (EqualLink (Time 3000) (Variable "$x")))
... etc....)

That is, you actually want the value to be present in the atomspace. This
is "implicitly obvious" and so pull req #590 now handles this automatically
(you are not the first to trip on this).

Using the PresentLink is .. optional, as it is implicitly assumed, as well;
so this will work also:

(BindLink ... etc....
   (AndLink
       (Variable "$x")
       (EqualLink (Time 3000) (Variable "$x")))
... etc....)

PresentLink stands in contrast [...]
